title: Fibromyalgia
section: Disorders of the Joints and Adjacent Tissues

items.title: Understanding the Core Characteristics of Fibromyalgia
items.content: Fibromyalgia (FM) is primarily a chronic pain syndrome marked by widespread musculoskeletal pain and tenderness. It also involves neuropsychological symptoms such as fatigue, unrefreshing sleep, cognitive dysfunction, anxiety, and depression. FM often coexists with other conditions like myalgic encephalitis/chronic fatigue syndrome, temporomandibular disorder, chronic headaches, irritable bowel syndrome, interstitial cystitis/painful bladder syndrome, and pelvic pain syndromes.
keyPoints: FM is characterized by widespread musculoskeletal pain and tenderness.
Associated symptoms include fatigue, unrefreshing sleep, cognitive dysfunction, anxiety, and depression.
FM coexists with other chronic primary or overlapping pain conditions.
Central nervous system involvement is key in maintaining FM symptoms.

items.title: Epidemiology of Fibromyalgia: Prevalence and Risk Factors
items.content: The worldwide prevalence of fibromyalgia is approximately 2%, with a higher prevalence in women (~4%) compared to men (<1%). The diagnosis is more common in clinical settings among women, with a ratio of ~8:1. In population studies, the gender ratio is closer to 3:1. FM prevalence increases significantly in patients with rheumatic diseases like rheumatoid arthritis or systemic lupus erythematosus, where up to 30% have comorbid FM. Risk factors include sleep disturbances, physical inactivity, and overweight or obesity.
keyPoints: Worldwide prevalence of FM is ~2%, with ~4% in women and <1% in men.
Diagnosis ratio in clinical settings: ~8:1 (women to men); population studies show 3:1.
Up to 30% of patients with rheumatic diseases have comorbid FM.
Risk factors include sleep disturbances, physical inactivity, and overweight or obesity.

items.title: Criteria for Diagnosing Fibromyalgia
items.content: Fibromyalgia is diagnosed based on widespread pain present most of the day, on most days, for at least three months. Widespread pain involves both sides of the body above and below the waist, including the axial skeleton.
keyPoints: Widespread pain must be present above and below the waist and involve the axial skeleton (neck, back, or chest).
Pain should have been present most of the day on most days for at least 3 months.
Newer diagnostic criteria focus on patient-reported symptoms rather than tender points.

items.title: Identifying Pain Sensitivity in Fibromyalgia
items.content: Fibromyalgia pain is associated with tenderness and increased evoked pain sensitivity. This can be identified by pressure-induced pain or a tender-point examination using approximately 4 kg/m^2 of pressure.
keyPoints: Pain sensitivity may be identified by pressure from a blood pressure cuff or skin roll tenderness.
Tender-point examination involves exerting ~4 kg/m^2 of pressure on specific musculotendinous sites.
Previously, 11 out of 18 tender points were required for diagnosis; newer criteria do not require this.

items.title: Neuropsychological Symptoms in Fibromyalgia
items.content: Patients with fibromyalgia often experience fatigue, stiffness, sleep disturbances, cognitive dysfunction, anxiety, and depression. These symptoms significantly impact function and quality of life.
keyPoints: Common neuropsychological symptoms include fatigue, stiffness, sleep disturbance, cognitive dysfunction, anxiety, and depression.
Fatigue is highly prevalent in primary care patients diagnosed with FM.
Lifetime prevalence of mood disorders in FM patients approaches 80%.

items.title: Understanding Comorbid Conditions in FM
items.content: Fibromyalgia (FM) frequently coexists with other chronic conditions, including musculoskeletal, infectious, metabolic, or psychiatric disorders. It is crucial to recognize that while FM affects ~2% of the general population, its prevalence rises to 10-30% among patients with degenerative or inflammatory rheumatic disorders due to their role as peripheral pain generators affecting central pain pathways.
keyPoints: FM affects ~2% of the general population.
Occurs in ~10-30% of patients with degenerative or inflammatory rheumatic disorders.
Comorbid conditions can alter central pain-processing pathways.
Clinicians should focus on treating central pain processes when FM emerges.

items.title: Psychosocial Considerations in FM Management
items.content: FM symptoms often begin or worsen during periods of perceived stress, suggesting an interaction between central stress physiology and pain-processing pathways. Understanding a patient's current psychosocial stressors is essential for management, as many exacerbating factors are not addressed by pharmacologic treatments. Additionally, a high prevalence of past interpersonal violence exposure in FM patients necessitates consideration of posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) in treatment plans.
keyPoints: FM symptoms can be exacerbated during periods of perceived stress.
Psychosocial stressors play a significant role in symptom management.
High prevalence of previous interpersonal and other forms of violence exposure.
Consideration of PTSD is important for comprehensive patient care.

items.subtopic: LABORATORY OR RADIOGRAPHIC TESTING | GENETICS AND PHYSIOLOGY
items.title: When and How to Use Imaging in Fibromyalgia Diagnosis
items.content: In fibromyalgia (FM), routine laboratory and radiographic tests typically yield normal results unless comorbidities are present. Radiographic testing should be used sparingly, primarily for diagnosing inflammatory arthritis. MRI of the spine is discouraged unless there are signs of inflammatory spine disease or neurologic symptoms.
keyPoints: Routine lab/radiographic tests in FM without comorbidities yield normal results.
Radiographic testing should be very sparing and mainly used for inflammatory arthritis diagnosis.
MRI of the spine is discouraged unless features suggest inflammatory spine disease or neurologic symptoms.
items.reference: Fibromyalgia · LABORATORY OR RADIOGRAPHIC TESTING | GENETICS AND PHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: LABORATORY OR RADIOGRAPHIC TESTING | GENETICS AND PHYSIOLOGY
items.title: Genetic and Physiological Insights into Fibromyalgia
items.content: Fibromyalgia is influenced by multiple genes, particularly those involved in pain and stress response pathways. Genetic factors overlap with other chronic pain conditions, involving serotonin metabolism and receptors. Psychophysical testing shows altered sensory processing and impaired inhibitory control, leading to hyperalgesia and allodynia.
keyPoints: Multiple genes contribute to FM vulnerability, especially those controlling pain and stress responses.
Genetic factors overlap with other chronic pain conditions, involving serotonin pathways.
Psychophysical testing reveals altered sensory processing and impaired inhibitory control in FM patients.
items.reference: Fibromyalgia · LABORATORY OR RADIOGRAPHIC TESTING | GENETICS AND PHYSIOLOGY

items.title: Role of Physical Activity in Fibromyalgia Management
items.content: Physical conditioning, including aerobic exercise and strength training, is crucial for managing fibromyalgia symptoms. Patients should start with low levels of activity and gradually increase intensity to improve function and reduce pain.
keyPoints: Exercise programs are the most helpful strategies for reducing tenderness and enhancing self-efficacy.
Patients who have been physically inactive may benefit from supervised or water-based programs initially.
Strength training can be recommended after achieving aerobic goals.

items.title: Cognitive-Behavioral Strategies in Fibromyalgia Management
items.content: Cognitive-behavioral strategies are effective for improving sleep hygiene and reducing illness behaviors, contributing to better management of fibromyalgia symptoms.
keyPoints: Focus on improved function and quality of life rather than elimination of pain.
Illness behaviors such as frequent physician visits should be discouraged.
Cognitive-behavioral strategies can help manage sleep hygiene and reduce illness behaviors.

items.title: Avoid Strong Opioids in FM Treatment
items.content: Strong opioid analgesics should not be used for fibromyalgia (FM) management due to lack of efficacy and potential for adverse effects that can worsen symptoms.
keyPoints: Opioid analgesics have no demonstrated efficacy in FM.
Use of opioids is associated with worsening symptoms and function.

items.title: Selecting Multi-Symptom Agents for FM
items.content: For fibromyalgia patients, using single agents that target multiple symptom domains (e.g., pain and sleep disturbance) is recommended. Examples include cyclobenzaprine, sedating antidepressants like amitriptyline, and alpha-2-delta ligands such as gabapentin and pregabalin.
keyPoints: Use of single agents for multiple symptoms is encouraged.
Examples: cyclobenzaprine, amitriptyline, gabapentin, pregabalin.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a commonly reported symptom associated with fibromyalgia?
items.explanation: Hypertension is not listed as a commonly reported symptom associated with fibromyalgia.

items.question: What is the approximate worldwide prevalence of fibromyalgia?
items.explanation: The passage states that the worldwide prevalence of fibromyalgia is ~2%.

items.question: In clinical settings, what is the approximate ratio of women to men diagnosed with fibromyalgia?
items.explanation: The passage indicates that in clinical settings, the diagnosis of FM is far more common in women than in men, with a ratio of ~8:1.

items.question: Which of the following best describes the operational definition of widespread pain in fibromyalgia?
options: Pain localized to one side of the body
Pain present above and below the waist on both sides of the body, involving the axial skeleton
Localized pain that is easily ignored
items.explanation: Widespread pain in FM involves being present both above and below the waist on both sides of the body and involving the axial skeleton.

items.question: For a diagnosis of fibromyalgia, how long must pain have been present?
items.explanation: Pain should have been present most of the day on most days for at least 3 months.

items.question: Which neuropsychological symptom is NOT commonly reported by patients with fibromyalgia?
items.explanation: Patients typically report fatigue, stiffness, and sleep disturbance; hyperactivity is not commonly reported.

items.question: What is a common challenge in diagnosing fibromyalgia?
options: Distinguishing FM pain from ongoing pain of triggering conditions
Identifying the exact location of pain
Determining if pain is present only during physical activity
Assessing whether pain can be ignored
items.explanation: A major challenge is to distinguish the ongoing pain of a triggering condition from FM pain.

items.question: Which percentage range best represents the occurrence of fibromyalgia in patients with degenerative or inflammatory rheumatic disorders?
items.explanation: FM occurs in ~10-30% of patients with degenerative or inflammatory rheumatic disorders.

items.question: What should clinicians focus on when FM emerges, characterized by pain outside the boundaries of what could reasonably be explained by a triggering condition?
options: Treatment of peripheral or inflammatory causes
items.explanation: When FM emerges, treatment should focus on central pain processes.

items.question: What is important for clinicians to consider when managing patients with fibromyalgia?
items.explanation: Understanding current psychosocial stressors aids in patient management.

items.subtopic: LABORATORY OR RADIOGRAPHIC TESTING | GENETICS AND PHYSIOLOGY
items.question: Which imaging test is discouraged for patients with fibromyalgia unless specific symptoms are present?
options: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the spine
items.explanation: MRI of the spine is discouraged unless there are features suggesting inflammatory spine disease or neurologic symptoms.
items.reference: Fibromyalgia · LABORATORY OR RADIOGRAPHIC TESTING | GENETICS AND PHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: LABORATORY OR RADIOGRAPHIC TESTING | GENETICS AND PHYSIOLOGY
items.question: What is a common finding in routine laboratory and radiographic tests for fibromyalgia without comorbidities?
items.explanation: Routine laboratory and radiographic tests yield normal results in FM without comorbidities.
items.reference: Fibromyalgia · LABORATORY OR RADIOGRAPHIC TESTING | GENETICS AND PHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: LABORATORY OR RADIOGRAPHIC TESTING | GENETICS AND PHYSIOLOGY
items.question: Which genetic pathways are implicated in the development of fibromyalgia?
options: Pathways controlling pain and stress responses
items.explanation: Genes associated with FM appear to be in pathways controlling pain and stress responses.
items.reference: Fibromyalgia · LABORATORY OR RADIOGRAPHIC TESTING | GENETICS AND PHYSIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a recommended nonpharmacologic treatment for fibromyalgia according to the passage?
options: Supervised or water-based programs for physically inactive patients
Frequent physician visits to manage symptoms
Meditative movement therapies such as yoga or Tai Chi
items.explanation: The passage discourages frequent physician visits and encourages behaviors that focus on improved function.

items.question: What is the primary goal of treatment for fibromyalgia as described in the passage?
options: Improved function and quality of life
items.explanation: The physician should focus on improved function and quality of life rather than elimination of pain.

items.question: Which device has been approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration for use in fibromyalgia treatment?
items.explanation: The passage states that the U.S. Food and Drug Administration has approved devices including a laser therapy device and a TENS device.

items.question: Which of the following medications is generally discouraged for use in fibromyalgia (FM) due to opioid-related adverse effects?
items.explanation: Tramadol has been studied but is generally discouraged in FM due to opioid-related adverse effects.

items.question: Which class of drugs should be avoided in patients with fibromyalgia due to lack of efficacy and potential worsening of symptoms?
items.explanation: Strong opioid analgesics have no demonstrated efficacy in FM and can worsen symptoms.

items.question: Which medication is recommended for fibromyalgia patients whose pain is associated with fatigue, anxiety, or depression due to its dual effects?
items.explanation: Duloxetine has both analgesic and antidepressant/anxiolytic effects, making it suitable for such patients.

items.statement: The prevalence of fibromyalgia varies significantly across different world regions.
items.explanation: The passage states that the prevalence data are similar across world regions.

items.statement: Patients with fibromyalgia often have comorbid rheumatic diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that the prevalence of FM is much higher in patients with rheumatic diseases like rheumatoid arthritis.

items.statement: The newer criteria for diagnosing fibromyalgia require identification of tender points.
items.explanation: Newer criteria eliminate the need for identification of tender points, focusing instead on patient-reported symptoms.

items.statement: Patients with fibromyalgia often have a history of arthritis or other inflammatory conditions.
items.explanation: Peripheral pain generators like arthritis can serve as triggers for the widespread pain in FM.

items.statement: An inflammatory or autoimmune etiology for fibromyalgia has been rigorously tested and confirmed.
items.explanation: The proposition of an inflammatory or autoimmune etiology for FM has not been rigorously tested.

items.statement: Functional assessment in fibromyalgia should include physical, mental, and social domains.
items.explanation: Functional assessment should indeed cover physical, mental, and social domains.

items.subtopic: LABORATORY OR RADIOGRAPHIC TESTING | GENETICS AND PHYSIOLOGY
items.statement: Functional MRI studies show no activation of brain regions involved in pain experience in FM patients.
items.explanation: Functional MRI demonstrates activation of brain regions involved in the experience of pain in response to innocuous stimuli in FM patients.
items.reference: Fibromyalgia · LABORATORY OR RADIOGRAPHIC TESTING | GENETICS AND PHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: LABORATORY OR RADIOGRAPHIC TESTING | GENETICS AND PHYSIOLOGY
items.statement: Genetic factors associated with serotonin and other monoamines are implicated in fibromyalgia.
items.explanation: Genes associated with metabolism, transport, and receptors of serotonin and other monoamines have been implicated in FM.
items.reference: Fibromyalgia · LABORATORY OR RADIOGRAPHIC TESTING | GENETICS AND PHYSIOLOGY

items.statement: Physical activity and exercise are found to be the least helpful strategies in managing fibromyalgia.
items.explanation: The passage states that physical activity and exercise are consistently found to be the most helpful strategies.

items.statement: Cognitive-behavioral strategies can improve sleep hygiene in fibromyalgia management.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that cognitive-behavioral strategies to improve sleep hygiene can be helpful.

items.statement: Glucocorticoids are effective against FM-related symptoms.
items.explanation: Glucocorticoids may be useful for inflammatory triggers but not for FM-related symptoms.

items.statement: Single agents that treat multiple symptom domains are discouraged in fibromyalgia treatment.
items.explanation: Use of single agents to treat multiple symptom domains is strongly encouraged.

items.assertion: Cognitive dysfunction is a neuropsychological symptom of fibromyalgia.
items.reason: Patients with FM experience difficulties with attention, word retrieval, and short-term memory loss.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; cognitive issues in FM include problems with attention and memory.

items.assertion: Depression is necessary for the diagnosis of fibromyalgia.
items.reason: Symptoms of anxiety and depression are common in patients with FM.
items.explanation: While symptoms of anxiety and depression are common, depression is neither necessary nor sufficient for diagnosing FM.

items.assertion: Fibromyalgia symptoms often have their onset during periods of perceived stress.
items.reason: This pattern may reflect an interaction among central stress physiology, vigilance or anxiety, and central pain-processing pathways.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.subtopic: LABORATORY OR RADIOGRAPHIC TESTING | GENETICS AND PHYSIOLOGY
items.assertion: Psychophysical testing has shown that fibromyalgia patients experience altered pain processing.
items.reason: This is due to impaired descending noxious inhibitory control leading to hyperalgesia and allodynia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Fibromyalgia · LABORATORY OR RADIOGRAPHIC TESTING | GENETICS AND PHYSIOLOGY

items.assertion: Tramadol is the best first choice for fibromyalgia treatment.
items.reason: It has mild serotonin-noradrenaline reuptake inhibitor activity and shows efficacy in FM.
items.explanation: Although Tramadol has shown some efficacy, it is generally discouraged due to opioid-related adverse effects.
